subject: Rheumatology
title: Septic arthritis
preambleMarkdown:
# Septic arthritis

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Septic arthritis is an infectious syndrome or organism-related topic in rheumatology.
- This article focuses on inflammation, autoimmunity, joints, systemic features, serology, imaging, and immunosuppression.
- The practical clinical priority is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Septic arthritis to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Septic arthritis is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Community-acquired, healthcare-associated, travel-related, device-related, or opportunistic infection.
- Localized, disseminated, recurrent, latent, or postinfectious presentation.
- Bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, toxin-mediated, or mixed infection.
- Uncomplicated disease, severe disease, sepsis, source-control need, or outbreak/public-health concern.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Septic arthritis depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In rheumatology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Septic arthritis.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Septic arthritis disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Septic arthritis should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Septic arthritis first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Septic arthritis, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever, localizing symptoms, exposure history, immune status, device or procedure history, and severity define the syndrome.
- Immunocompromised patients, neonates, pregnancy, and older adults may lack classic symptoms.
- Sepsis, airway threat, neurologic involvement, necrosis, and rapidly progressive disease change urgency.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Septic arthritis, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused rheumatology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Septic arthritis presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Septic arthritis with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Septic arthritis is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Septic arthritis should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize sepsis, obtain high-yield cultures when they will not delay care, and start empiric therapy when indicated.
- Narrow therapy once organism, susceptibility, source, and response are known.
- Add source control, isolation, prophylaxis, vaccination, contact tracing, or public-health notification when relevant.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Septic arthritis is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Start empiric antimicrobial therapy promptly when severe infection, sepsis, meningitis, necrotizing infection, or immunocompromised infection is suspected.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Drain abscesses, remove infected devices, debride necrotic tissue, decompress obstruction, or obtain diagnostic samples when source control is needed.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Septic arthritis, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Septic arthritis may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Septic arthritis patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Septic arthritis, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Septic arthritis in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Septic arthritis, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Septic arthritis does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Septic arthritis may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Septic arthritis, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Septic arthritis as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Do not delay antimicrobials in unstable sepsis.
- Cultures are most valuable before antibiotics when feasible, but stabilization comes first.
- Source control can matter as much as antimicrobial choice.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Septic arthritis draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

```text
Prosthetic valve: S. epidermidis

coli
Meningitis 	See Table 10-2.

Quick HIT
Common laboratory abnormalities include lymphocytosis and
elevated aminotransferases.

Quick HIT
CMV Mononucleosis
- Most commonly seen in sexually active adolescents/young
adults. Milder than EBV-associated IM.

- Characterized by fevers, chills, fatigue, headaches, and
frequently, splenomegaly
- Cervical lymphadenopathy and pharyngitis usually absent or
mild
- Negative for heterophile antibodies, frequently causes elevated
transaminases
C. Diagnosis

## 1. Serology

a. Monospot test-for detection of heterophile antibody
- Heterophile antibodies are positive within 4 weeks of
infection with EBV mononucleosis and are undetectable by 6
months. Thus, a positive monospot test indicates acute
infection with EBV mononucleosis
- Heterophile antibodies do not form in CMV mononucleosis
- Rapid heterophile tests are highly sensitive and specific,
particularly in adolescents
e. EBV-specific antibody testing-perform in cases in which
diagnosis is not straightforward (usually done by indirect
immunofluorescence microscopy or by ELISA)
2. Peripheral blood smear-usually reveals lymphocytic leukocytosis
with large, atypical lymphocytes
3. Throat culture-perform if pharyngitis is present to rule out a

secondary infection with beta-hemolytic streptococci
Quick HIT
If you suspect IM, order WBC count with differential and a
monospot test. If monospot test is positive, no further testing
needed. If negative and clinical suspicion is high, can repeat
monospot test.

D. Complications

## 1. Hepatitis

2. Neurologic complications (rare): meningoencephalitis, Guillain-
Barre syndrome, Bell palsy
3. Splenic rupture-rare
4. Thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia
5. Upper airway obstruction due to lymphadenopathy-rare
E. Treatment
1. In general, no specific treatment is indicated (or available) other
than supportive care, as most people recover completely within 3
to 4 months. Supportive care includes:
a. Rest, fluids
b. Avoidance of strenuous activities and contact sports until
splenomegaly resolves to prevent splenic rupture. This
precaution applies to all patients, even those without
splenomegaly on physical examination (low sensitivity for
detecting splenomegaly)
c. Analgesics to reduce temperature and pharyngeal pain-NSAIDs
or acetaminophen
2. Give a short course of corticosteroids and consult ENT if there is
airway compromise. Corticosteroids have also been effective in
patients with fulminant liver failure, severe thrombocytopenia, or
hemolytic anemia. They are not recommended for routine IM
without complications
3. When to return to sports-concern is splenic rupture. Patients
should wait 3 to 4 weeks from symptom onset before returning to
sports as splenic rupture is very rare after 4 weeks

Diseases of the Skin
and Hypersensitivity
Disorders 11
Mark D. Duncan
KEY DEFINITIONS
- Note: exact size definitions vary, but below are common definitions
used
- Dermatosis: any cutaneous lesion/group of lesions; nonspecific
term used to include any type of skin disease
- Dermatitis: inflammation of the skin
- Macule: circumscribed flat alterations in skin color <=1 cm
- Patch: a macule >1 cm
- Papule: solid, elevated lesion with no visible fluid <=0.5 cm
- Nodule: a single lesion (papule) >0.5 cm
- Plaque: discrete elevated lesions >0.5 cm, often formed by a
confluence of papules
- Vesicle: fluid-filled (serous fluid) lesions that are elevated and
<=0.5 cm
- Pustule: fluid-filled (cloudy/purulent material of necrotic
inflammatory cells)
- Bullae: vesicle >0.5 cm
- Wheal: rounded/flat-topped edematous elevation in the skin; often
erythematous
- Telangiectasia: visible, dilated blood vessel in the superficial skin
that blanches
- Petechiae: small (1 to 2 mm), nonblanching erythematous
macules that result from microhemorrhages
- Ecchymosis: nonblanching and nonpalpable red/purple
discoloration of the skin that results from extravasation of blood
into the skin
- Palpable purpura: palpable, nonblanching red/purple
discoloration of the skin that results from extravasation of blood
into the skin with an inflammatory etiology (i.e., vasculitis)

COMMON SKIN DISORDERS
Acneiform Eruptions
```

### Source Excerpt: Monarticular Arthritis

_Source lines: 19837-20246._

```text
V. MONARTICULAR ARTHRITIS
A. Discussion. Monarticular arthritis is characterized by pain and inflammation in
an isolated joint.

1. Septic arthritis and the crystal-induced arthropathies (i.e., gout, pseudogout)
are the key causes of acute monarticular arthritis that need to be addressed
in the ED. Joint infection can coexist with gout or pseudogout: Infection in a
joint with any microcrystalline deposition process can lead to crystal shedding
and subsequent synovitis from both crystals and microorganisms.

a. Septic arthritis may be bacterial, tuberculous, or fungal in etiology, and it has
the potential for rapid disease progression. Cases are usually classified as those
Quick 	HIT
Almost any joint disorder is
capable of presenting initially
as monarticular arthritis, but
usually, the practitioner can
identify patients who might
have an acute inflammatory
or infectious condition and
appropriately proceed with
vigorous evaluation and
treatment in the ED.

244 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Rheumatologic and Allergic Emergencies
that are caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae (disseminated gonococcal arthritis)
and those that are caused by all other organisms (nongonococcal arthritis).

i. Disseminated gonococcal arthritis. N. gonorrhoeae is the most common
cause of bacterial arthritis in urban centers. Disseminated gonococcal
arthritis tends to be a disease of young, sexually active, healthy adults.

Women, especially pregnant or menstruating women, are affected more
often than men.

ii. Nongonococcal arthritis
a) Causes. Staphylococcus aureus is the bacterium most frequently cul-
tured, followed by various species of streptococci. Other causative
organisms are Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa in elderly
patients and intravenous drug users and Haemophilus influenzae in
young children and, rarely, in adults. When joint infection is present
in patients with HIV, opportunistic and less common microorganisms,
such as Cryptococcus neoformans and Salmonella, have been identified.

b) Pathogenesis
1) An ongoing illness or problem may allow hematogenous spread
of bacteria to a joint. At-risk patients include those with im-
paired host defense mechanisms, those with indwelling venous
catheters, those with chronic arthritis (especially rheumatoid
arthritis), and intravenous drug users.

2) Alternatively, microorganisms may be directly introduced into the
joint through a deep penetrating wound (including human bite
and animal bite wounds), contiguous osteomyelitis, intra-articu-
lar injection or aspiration, arthroscopy, or prosthetic joint surgery.

b. Gout. In this condition, sodium urate crystals precipitate in and around the
joints of the extremities when the concentration of urate exceeds its solubility.

i. Patient history. Gout is more common in men than in women; when
it occurs in women, it is most often seen in postmenopausal women.

Patients may have a history of food overindulgence, heavy alcohol in-
take, use of medications (e.g., diuretics), or trauma. Obesity and hyper-
triglyceridemia are also associated with gout.

ii. Phases. In primary hyperuricemia, the tendency toward acute gout
increases with the serum urate level. In the natural history of gout, the
phases include:
a) Asymptomatic hyperuricemia (serum urate concentration greater
than 7 mg/dL)
b) Acute monarticular arthritis with few constitutional symptoms
c) Arthritis attacks that are polyarticular, associated with fever, and/
or occurring at shorter intervals
d) Tophi and chronic arthritis with superimposed exacerbations
iii. Associated problems can include renal disease and uric acid nephrolithiasis.

c. Pseudogout , which clinically resembles acute gouty monarthritis, is a form
of calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD). (More chronic
forms may resemble osteoarthritis, neuropathic arthropathy, rheumatoid
arthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis.) Surveys have shown the radiographic
incidence to be as high as 25% in some populations, but many of these
patients are asymptomatic.

i. Pathogenesis. Crystals of CPPD develop in cartilages and other connec-
tive tissue. The precise reasons for crystal deposition are not known.

ii. Patient history
a) CPPD increases in prevalence with age and is somewhat more preva-
lent in men. It is associated with hyperparathyroidism and hemo-
chromatosis, and CPPD crystalline deposits have been identified in
patients with hypothyroidism, urate gout, and Wilson disease, as
well as in kindreds without an identified specific metabolic defect.

b) An attack of pseudogout may be precipitated by trauma, surgical
procedures, or serious medical illness.

R H E U M A T O L O G I C A N D A L L E R G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	245

Rheumatologic and Allergic Emergencies
2. Other conditions. Acute monarticular arthritis may be the atypical presenta-
tion of a number of other inflammatory conditions that usually cannot be
definitively diagnosed in the ED.

a. If the patient does not appear ill, the monarticular arthritis may be a presenta-
tion of rheumatoid arthritis or one of the seronegative spondyloarthropathies.

b. If the patient shows signs of systemic illness, the patient may have entero-
pathic arthritis or systemic autoimmune disease.

B. Clinical features

## 1. General

a. Symptoms. The patient usually describes a short course of increasing pain,
redness, swelling, and immobility of a single joint, sometimes associated
with fever, skin lesions, or other symptoms and signs related to the specific
disease process.

b. Physical examination findings. Examination of the joint usually reveals
swelling, warmth, and/or redness that is not present on the contralateral
side. Effusion may be present.

2. Septic arthritis
a. Disseminated gonococcal infection. Twenty-five percent to 50% of patients
present with a single hot, swollen joint, whereas the others usually have mi-
gratory polyarthralgia or polyarthritis. Fever, dermatitis, and tenosynovitis
are the most common findings on the initial examination. The skin lesions
are usually small papules located on the trunk or extremities.

b. Nongonococcal bacterial arthritis. Patients may be febrile. The knee is
most often affected in adults, and the hip is most often affected in children.

Bacterial infection of the sternoclavicular and sacroiliac joints is associated
with intravenous drug use.

## 3. Gout

a. The typical gout attack begins suddenly, often at night, and within a few
hours the joint becomes visibly inflamed and exquisitely tender. The skin is
often tense and shiny. Swelling is related to synovial effusion and to periar-
ticular edema. The attacks usually subside within a few days to a few weeks,
even if untreated.

b. Gout typically occurs in the lower extremity, often confined to the first
metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra). Involvement of other joints of the
foot may occur simultaneously or in rapid succession. In the chronically
untreated hyperuricemic patient with recurrent attacks, there is involvement
of an increasing number of joints in addition to those of the feet (e.g., the
hands, wrists, ankles, knees, elbows). Tophi may be present if gout has been
untreated for approximately 10 years.

4. Pseudogout by far most commonly involves the knee joint. Attacks generally
last up to 10 days but may persist for months. Findings consistent with acute
synovitis are noted, and fever (as high as 40C) may occur.

C. Differential diagnoses
1. Septic arthritis, gout , and pseudogout are the prime diagnostic considerations
when a patient presents to the ED with a clinical picture consistent with
monarticular arthritis.

2. Periarticular problems. Localized periarticular processes (e.g., bursitis, tendi-
nitis, soft tissue infection, bone disease) may cause pain and swelling near a
single joint. Bone pain may be caused by Paget disease or osteomyelitis, or it
may be related to hemoglobinopathies, pulmonary hypertrophic osteoarthro-
pathy, or malignancy.

3. Noninflammatory monarticular arthritis
a. Structural joint problems may be related to trauma or overuse, internal de-
rangement, loose body, fracture, neuropathic joint, or osteonecrosis.

b. Hemarthrosis is a consideration in patients with bleeding disorders or in
those taking anticoagulants.

c. Congenital disorders. Pediatric patients may present with congenital dyspla-
sia of the hip, slipped capital femoral epiphysis, or osteochondritis dissecans.

246 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Rheumatologic and Allergic Emergencies
d. Osteoarthritis. Older patients with osteoarthritis fairly often present with
monarthritis of the knee, hip, or other joints.

4. Underlying inflammatory conditions. Monarticular arthritis may be part of the
pattern of exacerbation and remission of many of the rheumatologic diseases
that are usually classified as polyarticular, such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic
arthritis, Reiter syndrome, and systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Similarly,
enteropathic arthritis is more commonly polyarticular, but monarticular arthritis
may be associated with Whipple disease, intestinal bypass, ulcerative colitis,
and regional enteritis.

5. Rare or less obvious causes of monarticular arthritis. Hydroxyapatite deposi-
tion, crystal-induced arthropathies other than gout and pseudogout, sarcoid,
Lyme disease, and myriad other conditions may present as acute monarticular
arthritis. Furthermore, when a single joint is persistently inflamed, consider-
ation must be given to the possibilities of indolent infection caused by slow-
growing organisms, such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Sporotrichum schenckii,
or Candida , and tumors, particularly pigmented villonodular synovitis.

D. Evaluation
1. Laboratory studies
a. Rapid-turnaround blood tests that may contribute to the diagnosis include a
CBC, ESR, and uric acid and calcium levels. However, a patient with gout
may not have hyperuricemia at the time of the acute attack, and most pa-
tients with pseudogout do not have hypercalcemia.

b. If gonococcal arthritis is suspected, testing for sexually transmitted infec-
tions should be conducted. Genitourinary testing is positive in 80% of pa-
tients with disseminated gonococcal infection.

c. Blood cultures are drawn in patients with suspected septic arthritis. In non-
gonococcal bacterial arthritis, blood cultures are positive approximately 50%
of the time, although they are positive less than 20% of the time in patients
with disseminated gonococcal infection.

2. Imaging studies. Radiographs of the involved joint are usually obtained to rule
out fracture and bone disease and to assess for findings consistent with specific
forms of arthritis.

a. In septic arthritis, routine radiographs generally reveal only joint effusion.

A CT scan may be helpful in diagnosing infection of the sternoclavicular or
sacroiliac joints.

b. In early gout, radiographs are expected to be negative, but in chronic gout,
there may be erosions of bone of 5 mm or more in diameter.

c. In pseudogout, there may be radiographic evidence of calcinosis in cartilage
and other structures.

3. Arthrocentesis is performed to obtain a sample of the joint fluid for study. The
joint fluid is analyzed for cell count, glucose and protein levels, viscosity or
mucin clot, crystals, and Gram stain characteristics. It is cultured for bacteria
(aerobic and anaerobic), acid-fast bacilli, and fungi. Table 10-6 lists findings
typical of various conditions.

a. Septic arthritis
i. In disseminated gonococcal arthritis, N. gonorrhoeae is cultured in less
than 50% of purulent joints. The mean white blood cell (WBC) count is
over 50,000/mm3
.

ii. In nongonococcal bacterial arthritis, the mean synovial fluid WBC count
is approximately 100,000/mm3
, but initial counts may be in the same range
as inflammatory arthritis. The causative organism is cultured approximately
90% of the time. Low levels of glucose are present in approximately 50% of
patients with septic joints but can also occur in rheumatoid arthritis.

b. Gout. In patients with gout, synovial fluid aspirated early in the clinical
course of joint inflammation contains negatively birefringent needle-like
crystals in polymorphonuclear leukocytes (PMNs). The WBC count in the
synovial fluid is usually approximately 15,000/mm3 but occasionally is in
the range of 70,000/mm 3 .

Quick 	HIT
Many gout patients will have
normal uric acid levels at
time of the attack.

Quick 	HIT
Synovial lactate measure-
ment is very specific for
septic arthritis.

R H E U M A T O L O G I C A N D A L L E R G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	247

Rheumatologic and Allergic Emergencies

Suggested Empiric Therapy for Septic Arthritis
and Bursitis
TABLE 10-7
c. Pseudogout. The synovial fluid WBC count is usually less than 15,000/mm3
,
although the count may reach 70,000/mm3 or higher. In pseudogout, the
crystals are weakly positively birefringent and rhomboid in shape.

E. Therapy
1. Septic arthritis
a. Antibiotics. The selection of the initial antibiotic is based on the results
of the Gram stain and the leading organisms in the differential diagnosis.

Table 10-7 contains guidelines regarding empiric therapy.

Diagnosis 	Appearance 	Mucin Clot 	WBC/mm3
Sugar (% of

Normal 	Straw-colored clear 	Good 	"200 	!100

Traumatic arthritis 	Straw-colored bloody,
or yellow
Good 	!2,000
Rheumatoid arthritis 	Turbid 	Fair to poor 	5,000-50,000 	"50-75
Spondyloarthropathies 	Turbid 	Fair to poor 	5,000-50,000 	"50-75

WBC, white blood cell.

Synovial Fluid Characteristics	TABLE 10-6
248 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Rheumatologic and Allergic Emergencies
b. Drainage. Most rheumatologists recommend an initial trial of closed-needle
aspiration (arthrocentesis), once or twice daily, in all joints except the hips,
which should be managed with open drainage (arthrotomy).

c. Immobilization. A splint or cast is used to immobilize the joint in a position
of function. The patient should begin passive range-of-motion exercises as
soon as possible.

2. Crystal-induced arthritis. When a joint of the lower extremities is involved, a
cane or crutches may facilitate limited ambulation.

a. Oral NSAIDs are first-line therapy. Narcotic analgesics may be needed
adjunctively. Low-dose salicylates (less than 3 g/day) should be avoided be-
cause they increase hyperuricemia through renal mechanisms.

i. Indomethacin is the NSAID traditionally used to treat crystal-induced ar-
thritis. The first dose is 75 to 150 mg, followed by 50 mg every 8 hours.

Smaller doses are frequently effective (e.g., 50 mg initially and then 25 to
50 mg every 8 hours).

ii. Other NSAIDs, such as ibuprofen and naproxen, are also effective and
may have fewer side effects than indomethacin. In adults, ketorolac may
be administered, intravenously or intramuscularly, to initiate treatment.

b. Colchicine administration can abort acute gout attacks. However, colchi-
cine does not affect the course of rheumatoid arthritis and does not have a
predictable effect in pseudogout. The oral dose of colchicine is 0.6 mg every
1 to 2 hours for up to a maximum of 14 doses. Oral colchicine frequently
causes nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Significant improvement in signs
and symptoms is expected in 12 to 24 hours, at which point the dosage can
be tapered to 0.6 mg two to three times daily.

c. Prednisone may be employed for refractory cases of gout or pseudogout or
when the standard treatment regimens are contraindicated. Glucocorticoids
can be administered orally or parenterally.

d. Allopurinol or probenecid is used to treat hyperuricemia in patients with
gout after remission of the acute attack.

e. Joint aspiration seems to be therapeutic in patients with pseudogout.

F. 	Disposition

## 1. Discharge

a. Most patients with straightforward crystal-induced arthritis can be treated as
outpatients. Prescriptions are usually written to cover the first week of therapy.

Clinic follow-up is important to ensure resolution of the acute episode and also
to address underlying health issues and any recurrent episodes. Possible indica-
tions for admission are intractable pain or uncertainty regarding the diagnosis.

b. When a patient has an acutely inflamed joint but an evaluation not diagnos-
tic and not suspicious for infection, discharge home with cultures pending
is acceptable with close follow-up. Outpatient workup, such as serologic
tests or special radiographs, may be appropriate to initiate from the ED if
the institution is set up to do this.

2. Admission. Patients who have been diagnosed as having septic arthritis, as
well as those suspected of having septic arthritis, should be admitted to the
hospital.
```
